revision.text:
{{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017年8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyogo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]

[[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Buddhist sculptures]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Stone statues]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Roadside religious icons]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[ja:石仏]]

{{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017年8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyogo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]

Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.

[[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Buddhist sculptures]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Stone statues]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Roadside religious icons]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[ja:石仏]]

{{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017年8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}

[[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Buddhist sculptures]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Stone statues]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Roadside religious icons]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kkk]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[ja:石仏]]

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017年8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyogo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]

== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}

=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}

{{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017年8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyogo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} </gallery>

== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.

{{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017年8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyogo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} </gallery>

[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Buddhist sculptures]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Stone statues]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:Roadside religious icons]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[category:kk]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]][[category:kk]]
[[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[ja:石仏]]

{{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017年8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyogo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]

'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017年8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyogo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]

{{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群}} (1976)]]

attribution]][[Category:Stone Buddha statue]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Stone Buddha statue}}[[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing statue]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump]] [[Category:attempting to force efresh]] [[Category:bump 1-20 (2)]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[category{Stone Buddha statue]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[ja:石仏]]

{{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017年8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyogo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]

This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017年8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyogo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]

{{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017年8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}

Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}

{{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017年8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}

However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017年8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}

{{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017年8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}

=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}

{{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017年8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}

However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017年8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}
