{{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017年8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyogo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]

=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠}} in the left. {{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyogo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]

revision.text:
{{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyogo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]

revision.text:
{{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyogo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]

== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代|12=draft}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世|12=draft}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|12=draft}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者|12=draft}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東|12=draft}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地|12=draft}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|12=draft}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|12=draft}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖|12=draft}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger|12=draft}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|12=draft}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道|12=draft}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|12=draft}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|12=draft}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音|12=draft}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger|12=draft}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|12=draft}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔|12=draft}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|12=draft}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|12=draft}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿|12=draft}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏|12=draft}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|12=draft}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|12=draft}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|12=draft}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=draft}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏|12=draft}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム|12=draft}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群|12=draft}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|12=draft}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺|12=draft}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyogo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=draft}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅|12=draft}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園|12=draft}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]

revision.text:
{{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群|12=draft}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像|12=draft}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石|12=draft}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|12=draft}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|12=draft}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|12=draft}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏|12=draft}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏|12=draft}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群|12=draft}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟|12=draft}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟|12=draft}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟|12=draft}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|12=draft}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代|12=draft}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世|12=draft}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|12=draft}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者|12=draft}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東|12=draft}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地|12=draft}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|12=draft}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|12=draft}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖|12=draft}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger|2=de|3=Reichsapfel|4=ja|5=宝珠|6=fr|7=Orbe crucigère|1=Globus cruciger|12=draft}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|12=draft}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道|12=draft}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|12=draft}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|12=draft}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音|12=draft}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger|2=de|3=Reichsapfel|4=ja|5=宝珠|6=fr|7=Orbe crucigère|1=Globus cruciger|12=draft}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|12=draft}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔|12=draft}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|12=draft}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|12=draft}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿|12=draft}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏|12=draft}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|12=draft}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|12=draft}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|12=draft}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=draft}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏|12=draft}}

However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来|12=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|12=draft}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖|12=draft}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger|2=de|3=Reichsapfel|4=ja|5=宝珠|6=fr|7=Orbe crucigère|1=Globus cruciger|12=draft}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|12=draft}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道|12=draft}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|12=draft}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|12=draft}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音|12=draft}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger|2=de|3=Reichsapfel|4=ja|5=宝珠|6=fr|7=Orbe crucigère|1=Globus cruciger|12=draft}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|12=draft}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshintō|ja|庚申塔|12=draft}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|12=draft}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|12=draft}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿|12=draft}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏|12=draft}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|12=draft}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|12=draft}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=draft}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|12=draft}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=draft}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏|12=draft}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム|12=draft}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群|12=draft}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|12=draft}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺|12=draft}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyogo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=draft}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅|12=draft}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園|12=draft}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2011年4月}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|[[バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群|バーミヤン渓谷の石仏と石窟]]（1976年）]]
'''石仏'''（せきぶつ）とは、[[石]]に彫られた[[仏像]]である。日本ではかつて[[神仏習合]]が定着し、仏以外も篤く信仰する人が多かったため、[[道祖神]]などの石の神像も多く建てられた。その規模は[[寺院]]や[[神社]]の[[境内]]、[[路傍]]などで見られる小さな「野仏」から、[[臼杵磨崖仏]]、[[インド]]の[[アジャンター石窟群|アジャンター石窟]]、[[エローラ石窟]]、[[中国]]の[[雲岡石窟]]、[[龍門石窟]]などに代表されるような巨大な[[岩盤]]に彫られた[[磨崖仏]]まで多様である。以下は日本の石仏について記述する。 [[ファイル:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|大日如来像（[[臼杵磨崖仏]]）]]
== 概要 == [[古代]]から[[中世]]の日本では、[[関西地方]]を中心に[[権力者]]や[[修験者]]、[[僧侶]]などによって多く作られた。[[近世]]に至ると、素朴な信仰心を背景に一般庶民がその制作に携るようになる。[[関東]]では多様な表現での石仏が多く彫られた。現存する石仏の約80％位は[[江戸時代]]に制作されたものといわれている。現代においても[[墓地]]のほか、[[災害]]や[[事故]]・[[殺人事件]]などの現場では、慰霊のために[[地蔵菩薩]]が置かれることがある。
こうした石仏は信仰の対象とされたほか、地域の民俗や文化を伝える存在として研究されたり、写真集などが出版されてきた。一方で、[[過疎地]]の人口減少や信仰心の衰退に、土木工事や住宅建設などが加わり、石仏が撤去・破壊されることも多い<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017年8月4日（文化面）</ref>。
=== 石仏の種類 ===
*[[阿弥陀如来]] *[[地蔵菩薩]] いわゆる「お地蔵さん」と呼び親しまれているもので、[[手|右手]]に[[錫杖]]、[[左手]]に[[宝珠]]を持っているのが特徴。全国各地で見られる。[[立像]]と[[坐像]]があり、坊主頭で衣をまとっている姿が一般的。丸彫り、線彫り、浮き彫りなどの彫り方の違いがある。 *[[地蔵菩薩#六地蔵|六地蔵]] 六体からなる地蔵菩薩。地蔵菩薩は[[弥勒菩薩]]がこの世に現れるまでの無仏の間、[[六道]]（[[地獄 (仏教)|地獄]]・[[餓鬼]]・[[畜生]]・[[修羅道|修羅]]・[[人間]]・[[天|天上]]）へ救いの手を差し伸べるとされ、各道に各地蔵が派遣されているという考えに基づく。
*[[馬頭観音]] 路傍に置かれる石仏（観音像）で最もよく見られる。文字で刻まれることもある。 *[[千手観音]] 千の慈眼、千の[[慈悲]]で[[衆生]]を救う観音菩薩。[[合掌]]する両手のほかに、左右20手ずつの40の手を持ち、掌中に各一眼を有す。 *[[如意輪観音]] [[立て膝]]で[[頬]]に[[指]]を当てた姿態の坐像で、一面二手、四手、六手像が一般的である。[[宝珠]]と[[法輪]]を手に持っている。江戸時代中期以降は、女性の信仰の対象になることが多くなり、月待供養、念仏供養などの主尊として数多く造られるようになった。
*[[道祖神]] [[峠]]や村境あるいは[[橋]]のたもとなどに多く祀られ、別名「さえのかみ」とも呼ばれ、その起源は中国に求められる。外敵や[[疫病]]から民を護ってくれるという素朴な[[民間信仰]]から生まれた神である。その姿も多種多様であり仲睦まじい男女の姿として彫られているものもあり[[縁結び]]、子孫繁栄、[[交通安全]]の神としても信仰される。日本では[[長野県]][[安曇野]]地方など数多く見られる。
*[[庚申塔]] [[村]]や[[集落]]などの出入り口にあり、[[申]](さる)と[[太陽]]と[[月]]が描かれている。中国より伝来した[[道教]]に由来する[[庚申信仰]]に基づいて建てられた石塔である。[[干支]]で[[サル|猿]]に例えられるため、「見ざる、言わざる、聞かざる」の三猿を彫られることや庚申の祭神が[[神道]]では[[猿田彦神]]とされることから、猿田彦神が彫られることもある。特に旧[[相模国]]を中心とした地域では数多くの庚申塔が建立された。
== 著名な石仏 == * 福島県 - [[大悲山の石仏]] * 長野県 - [[万治の石仏]] * 京都府 - [[当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区]]は路傍に石仏が多数存在する地区で、[[浄瑠璃寺]]と[[岩船寺]]を結ぶルートは特にその密度が高い。 * 奈良県 - [[飛鳥の石造物]]、石位寺三尊石仏、[[頭塔#指定文化財|頭塔石仏]]、[[地獄谷石窟仏]]、[[春日山石窟仏]]、[[大野寺#文化財|大野寺弥勒磨崖仏]]、金屋の石仏 * 兵庫県 - [[羅漢寺 (加西市)]]、[[木津の磨崖仏]] * 大分県 - [[臼杵磨崖仏]]（[[特別史跡]]・[[国宝]]）、[[熊野磨崖仏]]、[[鍋山磨崖仏]]、[[元宮磨崖仏]]、[[天念寺|川中不動]]、[[菅尾磨崖仏]]、[[岩屋寺石仏]]、[[犬飼石仏]]、[[大分元町石仏]]、[[高瀬石仏]]、[[緒方宮迫東石仏]]、[[緒方宮迫西石仏]]、[[緒方宮迫石仏]]
== ギャラリー == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|[[カンボジア]]・ [[アンコール・トム]]石仏 File:Ajantha1.jpg|[[アジャンター石窟群]] File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|[[猿石]]（[[奈良県]][[高市郡]][[明日香村]]） File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|[[大野寺|大野寺弥勒磨崖仏]]（[[奈良県]][[宇陀市]][[大野寺]]） File:Gohyakurakan Hyogo-Kasai.jpg|[[羅漢寺 (加西市)|羅漢寺]]の五百羅漢 File:Tono Kizugawa Kyoto pref Japan09s3.jpg|[[当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|笑い仏]]（阿弥陀三尊、1299年作） </gallery>
==脚注== {{reflist}}
== 関連項目 == *[[石仏町]] *[[石仏駅]] *[[阿羅漢]] *[[古法華自然公園]] - 多くの石仏、磨崖仏、最近に彫られた[[羅漢]]などが多数安置され、石仏制作の体験もできる。
== 外部リンク == *[https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ 日本石仏協会]
{{Normdaten}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:せきふつ}} [[Category:仏像]] [[Category:石仏|*]] [[Category:路傍の神仏]]

{{Cite sources|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像|qq=draft|qq=draft}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代|qq=draft|qq=draft}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger|2=de|3=Reichsapfel|4=ja|5=宝珠|6=fr|7=Orbe crucigère|1=Globus cruciger|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger|2=de|3=Reichsapfel|4=ja|5=宝珠|6=fr|7=Orbe crucigère|1=Globus cruciger|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔|qq=draft|qq=draft}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム|qq=draft|qq=draft}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群|qq=draft|qq=draft}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺|qq=draft|qq=draft}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyogo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢|qq=draft|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]
{{translated page|ja|石仏|version=96005561|comment=Imported full ja history}}

'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist statues|qq=}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|qq=}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan|qq=}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsojin|qq=}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobutsu|qq=}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rock-cut Buddha statues|qq=}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Stone Buddhas|qq=}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ajanta Caves|qq=}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ellora Caves|qq=}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yungang Grottoes|qq=}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Longmen Grottoes|qq=}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Stone Buddhas|qq=}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japan|qq=}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medieval Japan|qq=}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|qq=}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|qq=}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cemeteries|qq=}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|qq=}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitabha Buddha|qq=}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|qq=}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shakujō|qq=}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger|2=de|3=Reichsapfel|4=ja|5=宝珠|6=fr|7=Orbe crucigère|1=Globus cruciger|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōju|13=User:Immanuelle/Globus cruciger|qq=}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Jizō|qq=}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/six realms of existence|qq=}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayagriva (Buddhism)|qq=}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thousand-armed Kannon|qq=}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoirin Kannon|qq=}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger|2=de|3=Reichsapfel|4=ja|5=宝珠|6=fr|7=Orbe crucigère|1=Globus cruciger|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wish-granting jewel|13=User:Immanuelle/Globus cruciger|qq=}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsojin|qq=}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-tō|qq=}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin faith|qq=}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko|qq=}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/three wise monkeys|qq=}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daibisan no Sekibutsu|qq=}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manji Stone Buddha|qq=}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|qq=}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stone Carvings|qq=}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|qq=}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Stone Buddhas|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Magaibutsu|qq=}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Angkor Thom|qq=}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ajanta Caves|qq=}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saruishi|qq=}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnōdera|qq=}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyōgo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} </gallery>

Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitabha Buddha|qq=}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|qq=}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shakujō|qq=}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger|2=de|3=Reichsapfel|4=ja|5=宝珠|6=fr|7=Orbe crucigère|1=Globus cruciger|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōju|13=User:Immanuelle/Globus cruciger|qq=}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Jizō|qq=}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/six realms of existence|qq=}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayagriva (Buddhism)|qq=}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thousand-armed Kannon|qq=}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoirin Kannon|qq=}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger|2=de|3=Reichsapfel|4=ja|5=宝珠|6=fr|7=Orbe crucigère|1=Globus cruciger|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wish-granting jewel|13=User:Immanuelle/Globus cruciger|qq=}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsojin|qq=}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-tō|qq=}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin faith|qq=}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko|qq=}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/three wise monkeys|qq=}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daibisan no Sekibutsu|qq=}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manji Stone Buddha|qq=}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|qq=}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stone Carvings|qq=}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|qq=}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Stone Buddhas|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Magaibutsu|qq=}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Angkor Thom|qq=}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ajanta Caves|qq=}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saruishi|qq=}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnōdera|qq=}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyōgo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishibutsu Station|qq=}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arhat|qq=}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kohouke Natural Park|qq=}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.

[[sn:Stone Buddha]] [[sn:tone Buddha]] [[ja:石仏]] [[en:Stone Buddha]]
<!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha-->

{{Unreferenced|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|qq=}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist statues|qq=}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|qq=}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan|qq=}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsojin|qq=}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobutsu|qq=}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rock-cut Buddha statues|qq=}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Stone Buddhas|qq=}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ajanta Caves|qq=}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ellora Caves|qq=}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yungang Grottoes|qq=}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Longmen Grottoes|qq=}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Stone Buddhas|qq=}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japan|qq=}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medieval Japan|qq=}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|qq=}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|qq=}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cemeteries|qq=}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|qq=}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitabha Buddha|qq=}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|qq=}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shakujō|qq=}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger|2=de|3=Reichsapfel|4=ja|5=宝珠|6=fr|7=Orbe crucigère|1=Globus cruciger|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōju|13=User:Immanuelle/Globus cruciger|qq=}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Jizō|qq=}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/six realms of existence|qq=}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayagriva (Buddhism)|qq=}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thousand-armed Kannon|qq=}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoirin Kannon|qq=}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger|2=de|3=Reichsapfel|4=ja|5=宝珠|6=fr|7=Orbe crucigère|1=Globus cruciger|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wish-granting jewel|13=User:Immanuelle/Globus cruciger|qq=}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsojin|qq=}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-tō|qq=}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin|lt=Kōshin|lt=Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin faith|qq=}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko|qq=}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/three wise monkeys|qq=}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daibisan no Sekibutsu|qq=}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manji Stone Buddha|qq=}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|qq=}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stone Carvings|qq=}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|qq=}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Stone Buddhas|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Magaibutsu|qq=}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Angkor Thom|qq=}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ajanta Caves|qq=}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saruishi|qq=}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnōdera|qq=}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyōgo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} </gallery>

== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishibutsu Station|qq=}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arhat|qq=}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kohouke Natural Park|qq=}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.

This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Stone Buddhas|qq=}})]]

However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitabha Buddha|qq=}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|qq=}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shakujō|qq=}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger|2=de|3=Reichsapfel|4=ja|5=宝珠|6=fr|7=Orbe crucigère|1=Globus cruciger|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōju|13=User:Immanuelle/Globus cruciger|qq=}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Jizō|qq=}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/six realms of existence|qq=}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayagriva (Buddhism)|qq=}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thousand-armed Kannon|qq=}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoirin Kannon|qq=}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger|2=de|3=Reichsapfel|4=ja|5=宝珠|6=fr|7=Orbe crucigère|1=Globus cruciger|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wish-granting jewel|13=User:Immanuelle/Globus cruciger|qq=}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsojin|qq=}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-tō|qq=}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin|lt=Kōshin|lt=Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin faith|qq=}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko|qq=}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/three wise monkeys|qq=}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daibisan no Sekibutsu|qq=}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manji Stone Buddha|qq=}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|qq=}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stone Carvings|qq=}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|qq=}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Stone Buddhas|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Magaibutsu|qq=}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Angkor Thom|qq=}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ajanta Caves|qq=}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saruishi|qq=}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnōdera|qq=}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyōgo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} </gallery>

== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishibutsu Station|qq=}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arhat|qq=}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kohouke Natural Park|qq=}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.

[[sn:Stone Buddha]] [[sn:tone Buddha]] [[ja:石仏]] [[en:Stone Buddha]]
<!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha-->

{{Unreferenced|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|ja|バーミヤン渓谷の文化的景観と古代遺跡群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley|qq=}} (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are {{ill|Buddhist statues|ja|仏像|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist statues|qq=}} carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|qq=}}. In {{ill|Japan|ja|日本|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japan|qq=}}, where the tradition of {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|ja|神仏習合|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinbutsu-shūgō|qq=}} (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as {{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsojin|qq=}} were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobutsu|qq=}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rock-cut Buddha statues|qq=}}, such as the {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Stone Buddhas|qq=}}, the {{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ajanta Caves|qq=}}, the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ellora Caves|qq=}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yungang Grottoes|qq=}} and {{ill|Longmen Grottoes|ja|龍門石窟|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Longmen Grottoes|qq=}}. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Stone Buddhas|qq=}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient Japan|ja|古代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ancient Japan|qq=}} to the {{ill|Medieval Japan|ja|中世|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Medieval Japan|qq=}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|qq=}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|qq=}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Edo period|qq=}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|qq=}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|cemeteries|ja|墓地|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/cemeteries|qq=}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|qq=}}.
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''{{ill|Amitabha Buddha|ja|阿弥陀如来|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amitabha Buddha|qq=}}''' * '''{{ill|Jizō Bosatsu|ja|地蔵菩薩|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jizō Bosatsu|qq=}}''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a {{ill|shakujō|ja|錫杖|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shakujō|qq=}} in the right hand and a {{ill|Hōju|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger|2=de|3=Reichsapfel|4=ja|5=宝珠|6=fr|7=Orbe crucigère|1=Globus cruciger|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōju|13=User:Immanuelle/Globus cruciger|qq=}} in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Jizō|qq=}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the {{ill|six realms of existence|ja|六道|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/six realms of existence|qq=}}, these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''{{ill|Hayagriva (Buddhism)|lt=Horse-headed Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayagriva (Buddhism)|qq=}}''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thousand-armed Kannon|qq=}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''{{ill|Nyoirin Kannon|ja|如意輪観音|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nyoirin Kannon|qq=}}''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a {{ill|wish-granting jewel|ja|宝珠|1=Globus cruciger|2=de|3=Reichsapfel|4=ja|5=宝珠|6=fr|7=Orbe crucigère|1=Globus cruciger|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/wish-granting jewel|13=User:Immanuelle/Globus cruciger|qq=}} and a wheel of law. * '''{{ill|Dōsojin|ja|道祖神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dōsojin|qq=}}''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-tō|qq=}}''' Steles associated with {{ill|Kōshin|lt=Kōshin|lt=Kōshin faith|ja|庚申信仰|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin faith|qq=}}, often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko|qq=}} or the {{ill|three wise monkeys|ja|三猿|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/three wise monkeys|qq=}}.
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – {{ill|Daibisan no Sekibutsu|ja|大悲山の石仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daibisan no Sekibutsu|qq=}} * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manji Stone Buddha|qq=}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|qq=}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stone Carvings|qq=}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|qq=}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – {{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|ja|臼杵磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Usuki Stone Buddhas|qq=}}, {{ill|Kumano Magaibutsu|ja|熊野磨崖仏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Magaibutsu|qq=}}
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|{{ill|Angkor Thom|ja|アンコール・トム|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Angkor Thom|qq=}} in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|{{ill|Ajanta Caves|ja|アジャンター石窟群|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ajanta Caves|qq=}} File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saruishi|qq=}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at {{ill|Ōnōdera|ja|大野寺|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōnōdera|qq=}} File:Gohyakurakan Hyōgo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} </gallery>

== See also == * {{ill|Ishibutsu Station|ja|石仏駅|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ishibutsu Station|qq=}} * {{ill|Arhat|ja|阿羅漢|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Arhat|qq=}} * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kohouke Natural Park|qq=}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
