<!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--> [[Category:Buddha statues]] [[Category:Stone Buddha statues]] [[Category:Wayside Deities and Buddhas]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11585154}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石仏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11585154]] Wikidata name: stone Buddha statue [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|[[:en:Buddhas of Bamiyan|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley]] (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are [[:en:Buddha in art|Buddhist statues]] carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=stone|WD=Q22731}}. In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], where the tradition of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]] were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobutsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rock-cut Buddha statues|1=magaibutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11239248}}, such as the [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], the [[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]], the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ellora Caves|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to エローラ石窟群|1=Ellora Caves|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q189616}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yungang Grottoes|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雲崗石窟|1=Yungang Grottoes|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q308805}} and [[:en:Longmen Grottoes|Longmen Grottoes]]. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ([[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]])]]
== Overview == From [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient Japan]] to the [[:en:Middle Ages|Medieval Japan]], numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏}}, and monks. By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in [[:en:Cemetery|cemeteries]] or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]].
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[:en:Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''[[:en:Amitābha|Amitabha Buddha]]''' * '''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]]''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a [[:en:Khakkhara|shakujō]] in the right hand and a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Jizō|qq=}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the [[:en:Six Paths|six realms of existence]], these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''[[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Horse-headed Kannon]]''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thousand-armed Kannon|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''[[:en:Cintāmaṇicakra|Nyoirin Kannon]]''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] and a wheel of law. * '''[[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]]''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-tō|qq=}}''' Steles associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Kōshin faith]], often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} or the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|three wise monkeys]].
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – [[:en:Daihisan Stone Buddhas|Daibisan no Sekibutsu]] * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manji Stone Buddha|qq=}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|1=Tōno Stone Buddha Area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487822}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stone Carvings|qq=}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|1=Rakan-ji Temple, Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608594}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], [[:en:Kumano Magaibutsu|Kumano Magaibutsu]]
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|[[:en:Angkor Thom|Angkor Thom]] in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|[[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]] File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saruishi|qq=}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at [[:en:Ōno-ji|Ōnōdera]] File:Gohyakurakan Hyōgo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} </gallery>

'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are [[:en:Buddha in art|Buddhist statues]] carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=stone|WD=Q22731}}. In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], where the tradition of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]] were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobutsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rock-cut Buddha statues|1=magaibutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11239248}}, such as the [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], the [[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]], the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ellora Caves|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to エローラ石窟群|1=Ellora Caves|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q189616}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yungang Grottoes|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雲崗石窟|1=Yungang Grottoes|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q308805}} and [[:en:Longmen Grottoes|Longmen Grottoes]]. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ([[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]])]]
== Overview == From [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient Japan]] to the [[:en:Middle Ages|Medieval Japan]], numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏}}, and monks. By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in [[:en:Cemetery|cemeteries]] or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]].
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[:en:Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''[[:en:Amitābha|Amitabha Buddha]]''' * '''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]]''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a [[:en:Khakkhara|shakujō]] in the right hand and a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Jizō|qq=}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the [[:en:Six Paths|six realms of existence]], these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''[[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Horse-headed Kannon]]''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thousand-armed Kannon|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''[[:en:Cintāmaṇicakra|Nyoirin Kannon]]''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] and a wheel of law. * '''[[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]]''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-tō|qq=}}''' Steles associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Kōshin faith]], often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} or the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|three wise monkeys]].
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – [[:en:Daihisan Stone Buddhas|Daibisan no Sekibutsu]] * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manji Stone Buddha|qq=}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|1=Tōno Stone Buddha Area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487822}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stone Carvings|qq=}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|1=Rakan-ji Temple, Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608594}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], [[:en:Kumano Magaibutsu|Kumano Magaibutsu]]
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|[[:en:Angkor Thom|Angkor Thom]] in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|[[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]] File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saruishi|qq=}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at [[:en:Ōno-ji|Ōnōdera]] File:Gohyakurakan Hyōgo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[:en:Ishibotoke Station|Ishibutsu Station]] * [[:en:Arhat|Arhat]] * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kohouke Natural Park|1=Furubokke Nature Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11411414}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石仏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11585154]] Wikidata name: stone Buddha statue [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|[[:en:Buddhas of Bamiyan|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley]] (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are [[:en:Buddha in art|Buddhist statues]] carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=stone|WD=Q22731}}. In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], where the tradition of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]] were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobutsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rock-cut Buddha statues|1=magaibutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11239248}}, such as the [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], the [[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]], the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ellora Caves|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to エローラ石窟群|1=Ellora Caves|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q189616}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yungang Grottoes|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雲崗石窟|1=Yungang Grottoes|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q308805}} and [[:en:Longmen Grottoes|Longmen Grottoes]]. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ([[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]])]]
== Overview == From [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient Japan]] to the [[:en:Middle Ages|Medieval Japan]], numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏}}, and monks. By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in [[:en:Cemetery|cemeteries]] or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]].
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[:en:Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''[[:en:Amitābha|Amitabha Buddha]]''' * '''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]]''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a [[:en:Khakkhara|shakujō]] in the right hand and a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Jizō|qq=}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the [[:en:Six Paths|six realms of existence]], these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''[[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Horse-headed Kannon]]''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thousand-armed Kannon|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''[[:en:Cintāmaṇicakra|Nyoirin Kannon]]''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] and a wheel of law. * '''[[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]]''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-tō|qq=}}''' Steles associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Kōshin faith]], often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} or the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|three wise monkeys]].
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – [[:en:Daihisan Stone Buddhas|Daibisan no Sekibutsu]] * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manji Stone Buddha|qq=}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|1=Tōno Stone Buddha Area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487822}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stone Carvings|qq=}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|1=Rakan-ji Temple, Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608594}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], [[:en:Kumano Magaibutsu|Kumano Magaibutsu]]
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|[[:en:Angkor Thom|Angkor Thom]] in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|[[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]] File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saruishi|qq=}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at [[:en:Ōno-ji|Ōnōdera]] File:Gohyakurakan Hyōgo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} </gallery>

'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are [[:en:Buddha in art|Buddhist statues]] carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=stone|WD=Q22731}}. In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], where the tradition of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]] were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobutsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rock-cut Buddha statues|1=magaibutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11239248}}, such as the [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], the [[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]], the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ellora Caves|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to エローラ石窟群|1=Ellora Caves|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q189616}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yungang Grottoes|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雲崗石窟|1=Yungang Grottoes|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q308805}} and [[:en:Longmen Grottoes|Longmen Grottoes]]. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ([[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]])]]
== Overview == From [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient Japan]] to the [[:en:Middle Ages|Medieval Japan]], numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏}}, and monks. By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in [[:en:Cemetery|cemeteries]] or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]].
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[:en:Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''[[:en:Amitābha|Amitabha Buddha]]''' * '''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]]''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a [[:en:Khakkhara|shakujō]] in the right hand and a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Jizō|qq=}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the [[:en:Six Paths|six realms of existence]], these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''[[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Horse-headed Kannon]]''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thousand-armed Kannon|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''[[:en:Cintāmaṇicakra|Nyoirin Kannon]]''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] and a wheel of law. * '''[[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]]''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-tō|qq=}}''' Steles associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Kōshin faith]], often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} or the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|three wise monkeys]].
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – [[:en:Daihisan Stone Buddhas|Daibisan no Sekibutsu]] * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manji Stone Buddha|qq=}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|1=Tōno Stone Buddha Area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487822}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stone Carvings|qq=}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|1=Rakan-ji Temple, Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608594}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], [[:en:Kumano Magaibutsu|Kumano Magaibutsu]]
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|[[:en:Angkor Thom|Angkor Thom]] in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|[[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]] File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saruishi|qq=}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at [[:en:Ōno-ji|Ōnōdera]] File:Gohyakurakan Hyōgo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[:en:Ishibotoke Station|Ishibutsu Station]] * [[:en:Arhat|Arhat]] * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kohouke Natural Park|1=Furubokke Nature Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11411414}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]
{{translated page|ja|石仏|version=96005561|comment=Imported full ja history}}

=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''[[:en:Amitābha|Amitabha Buddha]]''' * '''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]]''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a [[:en:Khakkhara|shakujō]] in the right hand and a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Jizō|qq=}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the [[:en:Six Paths|six realms of existence]], these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''[[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Horse-headed Kannon]]''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thousand-armed Kannon|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''[[:en:Cintāmaṇicakra|Nyoirin Kannon]]''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] and a wheel of law. * '''[[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]]''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-tō|qq=}}''' Steles associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Kōshin faith]], often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} or the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|three wise monkeys]].
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – [[:en:Daihisan Stone Buddhas|Daibisan no Sekibutsu]] * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manji Stone Buddha|qq=}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|1=Tōno Stone Buddha Area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487822}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stone Carvings|qq=}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|1=Rakan-ji Temple, Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608594}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], [[:en:Kumano Magaibutsu|Kumano Magaibutsu]]
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|[[:en:Angkor Thom|Angkor Thom]] in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|[[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]] File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saruishi|qq=}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at [[:en:Ōno-ji|Ōnōdera]] File:Gohyakurakan Hyōgo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[:en:Ishibotoke Station|Ishibutsu Station]] * [[:en:Arhat|Arhat]] * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kohouke Natural Park|1=Furubokke Nature Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11411414}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石仏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11585154]] Wikidata name: stone Buddha statue [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|[[:en:Buddhas of Bamiyan|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley]] (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are [[:en:Buddha in art|Buddhist statues]] carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=stone|WD=Q22731}}. In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], where the tradition of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]] were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobutsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rock-cut Buddha statues|1=magaibutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11239248}}, such as the [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], the [[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]], the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ellora Caves|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to エローラ石窟群|1=Ellora Caves|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q189616}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yungang Grottoes|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雲崗石窟|1=Yungang Grottoes|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q308805}} and [[:en:Longmen Grottoes|Longmen Grottoes]]. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ([[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]])]]
== Overview == From [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient Japan]] to the [[:en:Middle Ages|Medieval Japan]], numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏}}, and monks. By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in [[:en:Cemetery|cemeteries]] or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]].
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[:en:Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''[[:en:Amitābha|Amitabha Buddha]]''' * '''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]]''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a [[:en:Khakkhara|shakujō]] in the right hand and a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Jizō|qq=}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the [[:en:Six Paths|six realms of existence]], these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''[[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Horse-headed Kannon]]''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thousand-armed Kannon|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''[[:en:Cintāmaṇicakra|Nyoirin Kannon]]''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] and a wheel of law. * '''[[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]]''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-tō|qq=}}''' Steles associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Kōshin faith]], often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} or the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|three wise monkeys]].
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – [[:en:Daihisan Stone Buddhas|Daibisan no Sekibutsu]] * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manji Stone Buddha|qq=}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|1=Tōno Stone Buddha Area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487822}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stone Carvings|qq=}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|1=Rakan-ji Temple, Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608594}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], [[:en:Kumano Magaibutsu|Kumano Magaibutsu]]
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|[[:en:Angkor Thom|Angkor Thom]] in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|[[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]] File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saruishi|qq=}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at [[:en:Ōno-ji|Ōnōdera]] File:Gohyakurakan Hyōgo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[:en:Ishibotoke Station|Ishibutsu Station]] * [[:en:Arhat|Arhat]] * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kohouke Natural Park|1=Furubokke Nature Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11411414}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石仏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11585154]] Wikidata name: stone Buddha statue [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|[[:en:Buddhas of Bamiyan|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley]] (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are [[:en:Buddha in art|Buddhist statues]] carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=stone|WD=Q22731}}. In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], where the tradition of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]] were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobutsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rock-cut Buddha statues|1=magaibutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11239248}}, such as the [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], the [[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]], the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ellora Caves|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to エローラ石窟群|1=Ellora Caves|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q189616}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yungang Grottoes|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雲崗石窟|1=Yungang Grottoes|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q308805}} and [[:en:Longmen Grottoes|Longmen Grottoes]]. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ([[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]])]]
== Overview == From [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient Japan]] to the [[:en:Middle Ages|Medieval Japan]], numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏}}, and monks. By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in [[:en:Cemetery|cemeteries]] or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]].
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[:en:Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''[[:en:Amitābha|Amitabha Buddha]]''' * '''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]]''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a [[:en:Khakkhara|shakujō]] in the right hand and a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Jizō|qq=}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the [[:en:Six Paths|six realms of existence]], these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''[[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Horse-headed Kannon]]''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thousand-armed Kannon|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''[[:en:Cintāmaṇicakra|Nyoirin Kannon]]''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] and a wheel of law. * '''[[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]]''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-tō|qq=}}''' Steles associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Kōshin faith]], often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} or the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|three wise monkeys]].
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – [[:en:Daihisan Stone Buddhas|Daibisan no Sekibutsu]] * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manji Stone Buddha|qq=}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|1=Tōno Stone Buddha Area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487822}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stone Carvings|qq=}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|1=Rakan-ji Temple, Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608594}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], [[:en:Kumano Magaibutsu|Kumano Magaibutsu]]
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|[[:en:Angkor Thom|Angkor Thom]] in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|[[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]] File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saruishi|qq=}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at [[:en:Ōno-ji|Ōnōdera]] File:Gohyakurakan Hyōgo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[:en:Ishibotoke Station|Ishibutsu Station]] * [[:en:Arhat|Arhat]] * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kohouke Natural Park|1=Furubokke Nature Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11411414}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]

== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – [[:en:Daihisan Stone Buddhas|Daibisan no Sekibutsu]] * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manji Stone Buddha|qq=}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|1=Tōno Stone Buddha Area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487822}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stone Carvings|qq=}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|1=Rakan-ji Temple, Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608594}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], [[:en:Kumano Magaibutsu|Kumano Magaibutsu]]
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|[[:en:Angkor Thom|Angkor Thom]] in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|[[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]] File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saruishi|qq=}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at [[:en:Ōno-ji|Ōnōdera]] File:Gohyakurakan Hyōgo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[:en:Ishibotoke Station|Ishibutsu Station]] * [[:en:Arhat|Arhat]] * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kohouke Natural Park|1=Furubokke Nature Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11411414}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.

<!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--><!--Stone Buddha--> [[Category:Buddha statues]] [[Category:Stone Buddha statues]] [[Category:Wayside Deities and Buddhas]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] {{wikidata link|Q11585154}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石仏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石仏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石仏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11585154]] Wikidata name: stone Buddha statue [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|[[:en:Buddhas of Bamiyan|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley]] (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are [[:en:Buddha in art|Buddhist statues]] carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=stone|WD=Q22731}}. In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], where the tradition of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]] were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobutsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rock-cut Buddha statues|1=magaibutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11239248}}, such as the [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], the [[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]], the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ellora Caves|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to エローラ石窟群|1=Ellora Caves|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q189616}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yungang Grottoes|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雲崗石窟|1=Yungang Grottoes|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q308805}} and [[:en:Longmen Grottoes|Longmen Grottoes]]. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ([[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]])]]
== Overview == From [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient Japan]] to the [[:en:Middle Ages|Medieval Japan]], numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏}}, and monks. By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in [[:en:Cemetery|cemeteries]] or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]].
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[:en:Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''[[:en:Amitābha|Amitabha Buddha]]''' * '''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]]''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a [[:en:Khakkhara|shakujō]] in the right hand and a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Jizō|qq=}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the [[:en:Six Paths|six realms of existence]], these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''[[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Horse-headed Kannon]]''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thousand-armed Kannon|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''[[:en:Cintāmaṇicakra|Nyoirin Kannon]]''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] and a wheel of law. * '''[[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]]''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-tō|qq=}}''' Steles associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Kōshin faith]], often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} or the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|three wise monkeys]].
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – [[:en:Daihisan Stone Buddhas|Daibisan no Sekibutsu]] * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manji Stone Buddha|qq=}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|1=Tōno Stone Buddha Area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487822}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stone Carvings|qq=}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|1=Rakan-ji Temple, Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608594}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], [[:en:Kumano Magaibutsu|Kumano Magaibutsu]]
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|[[:en:Angkor Thom|Angkor Thom]] in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|[[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]] File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saruishi|qq=}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at [[:en:Ōno-ji|Ōnōdera]] File:Gohyakurakan Hyōgo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} </gallery>

'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are [[:en:Buddha in art|Buddhist statues]] carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=stone|WD=Q22731}}. In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], where the tradition of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]] were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobutsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rock-cut Buddha statues|1=magaibutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11239248}}, such as the [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], the [[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]], the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ellora Caves|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to エローラ石窟群|1=Ellora Caves|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q189616}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yungang Grottoes|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雲崗石窟|1=Yungang Grottoes|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q308805}} and [[:en:Longmen Grottoes|Longmen Grottoes]]. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ([[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]])]]
== Overview == From [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient Japan]] to the [[:en:Middle Ages|Medieval Japan]], numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏}}, and monks. By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in [[:en:Cemetery|cemeteries]] or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]].
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[:en:Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''[[:en:Amitābha|Amitabha Buddha]]''' * '''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]]''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a [[:en:Khakkhara|shakujō]] in the right hand and a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Jizō|qq=}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the [[:en:Six Paths|six realms of existence]], these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''[[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Horse-headed Kannon]]''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thousand-armed Kannon|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''[[:en:Cintāmaṇicakra|Nyoirin Kannon]]''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] and a wheel of law. * '''[[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]]''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-tō|qq=}}''' Steles associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Kōshin faith]], often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} or the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|three wise monkeys]].
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – [[:en:Daihisan Stone Buddhas|Daibisan no Sekibutsu]] * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manji Stone Buddha|qq=}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|1=Tōno Stone Buddha Area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487822}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stone Carvings|qq=}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|1=Rakan-ji Temple, Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608594}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], [[:en:Kumano Magaibutsu|Kumano Magaibutsu]]
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|[[:en:Angkor Thom|Angkor Thom]] in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|[[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]] File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saruishi|qq=}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at [[:en:Ōno-ji|Ōnōdera]] File:Gohyakurakan Hyōgo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} </gallery>

== See also == * [[:en:Ishibotoke Station|Ishibutsu Station]] * [[:en:Arhat|Arhat]] * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kohouke Natural Park|1=Furubokke Nature Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11411414}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.

== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – [[:en:Daihisan Stone Buddhas|Daibisan no Sekibutsu]] * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manji Stone Buddha|qq=}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|1=Tōno Stone Buddha Area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487822}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stone Carvings|qq=}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|1=Rakan-ji Temple, Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608594}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], [[:en:Kumano Magaibutsu|Kumano Magaibutsu]]
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|[[:en:Angkor Thom|Angkor Thom]] in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|[[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]] File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saruishi|qq=}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at [[:en:Ōno-ji|Ōnōdera]] File:Gohyakurakan Hyōgo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[:en:Ishibotoke Station|Ishibutsu Station]] * [[:en:Arhat|Arhat]] * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kohouke Natural Park|1=Furubokke Nature Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11411414}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石仏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石仏]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:石仏]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11585154]] Wikidata name: stone Buddha statue [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:32, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Unreferenced|date=April 2011}} [[File:Afghanistan Statua di Budda 2.jpg|thumb|200px|right|[[:en:Buddhas of Bamiyan|Cultural Landscape and Archaeological Remains of the Bamiyan Valley]] (1976)]]
'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are [[:en:Buddha in art|Buddhist statues]] carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=stone|WD=Q22731}}. In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], where the tradition of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]] were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobutsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rock-cut Buddha statues|1=magaibutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11239248}}, such as the [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], the [[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]], the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ellora Caves|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to エローラ石窟群|1=Ellora Caves|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q189616}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yungang Grottoes|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雲崗石窟|1=Yungang Grottoes|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q308805}} and [[:en:Longmen Grottoes|Longmen Grottoes]]. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ([[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]])]]
== Overview == From [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient Japan]] to the [[:en:Middle Ages|Medieval Japan]], numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏}}, and monks. By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in [[:en:Cemetery|cemeteries]] or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]].
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[:en:Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''[[:en:Amitābha|Amitabha Buddha]]''' * '''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]]''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a [[:en:Khakkhara|shakujō]] in the right hand and a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Jizō|qq=}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the [[:en:Six Paths|six realms of existence]], these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''[[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Horse-headed Kannon]]''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thousand-armed Kannon|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''[[:en:Cintāmaṇicakra|Nyoirin Kannon]]''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] and a wheel of law. * '''[[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]]''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-tō|qq=}}''' Steles associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Kōshin faith]], often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} or the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|three wise monkeys]].
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – [[:en:Daihisan Stone Buddhas|Daibisan no Sekibutsu]] * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manji Stone Buddha|qq=}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|1=Tōno Stone Buddha Area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487822}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stone Carvings|qq=}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|1=Rakan-ji Temple, Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608594}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], [[:en:Kumano Magaibutsu|Kumano Magaibutsu]]
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|[[:en:Angkor Thom|Angkor Thom]] in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|[[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]] File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saruishi|qq=}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at [[:en:Ōno-ji|Ōnōdera]] File:Gohyakurakan Hyōgo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} </gallery>

== See also == * [[:en:Ishibotoke Station|Ishibutsu Station]] * [[:en:Arhat|Arhat]] * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kohouke Natural Park|1=Furubokke Nature Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11411414}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.

'''Sekibutsu''' (石仏) are [[:en:Buddha in art|Buddhist statues]] carved into {{ill|stone|ja|石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/stone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=stone|WD=Q22731}}. In [[:en:Japan|Japan]], where the tradition of [[:en:Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu-shūgō]] (the syncretism of Buddhism and Shinto) was well-established, stone icons of gods such as [[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]] were also commonly erected. These statues vary in size and context, from small roadside {{ill|Nobutsu|ja|野仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nobutsu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to grand-scale {{ill|Rock-cut Buddha statues|ja|磨崖仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rock-cut Buddha statues|1=magaibutsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11239248}}, such as the [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], the [[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]], the {{ill|Ellora Caves|ja|エローラ石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ellora Caves|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to エローラ石窟群|1=Ellora Caves|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q189616}}, and China’s {{ill|Yungang Grottoes|ja|雲岡石窟|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yungang Grottoes|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 雲崗石窟|1=Yungang Grottoes|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q308805}} and [[:en:Longmen Grottoes|Longmen Grottoes]]. This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ([[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]])]]
== Overview == From [[:en:Ancient history|Ancient Japan]] to the [[:en:Middle Ages|Medieval Japan]], numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏}}, and monks. By the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in [[:en:Cemetery|cemeteries]] or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of [[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]].
Sekibutsu are studied as cultural and folkloric artifacts, and photographic collections of them have been published. However, the decline in faith, depopulation in rural areas, and construction projects have led to the removal or destruction of many Sekibutsu.<ref>辻尾榮一「石仏こつこつ捜索隊◇路傍にたたずむ石造品を記録、毎月の例会は500回超え◇」『[[:en:Nikkei|日本経済新聞]]』朝刊2017th year 8月4日（文化面）</ref>
=== Types of Sekibutsu === * '''[[:en:Amitābha|Amitabha Buddha]]''' * '''[[:en:Kṣitigarbha|Jizō Bosatsu]]''' Commonly referred to as “Ojizō-san,” this statue typically features a bald monk in robes holding a [[:en:Khakkhara|shakujō]] in the right hand and a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] in the left. Found nationwide, Jizō statues may be standing or seated and carved in relief, line engraving, or full sculpture. * '''{{ill|Six Jizō|ja|六地蔵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Six Jizō|qq=}}''' Representing Jizō’s role as the savior of the [[:en:Six Paths|six realms of existence]], these six statues each oversee one realm. * '''[[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Horse-headed Kannon]]''' Often found along roadsides, sometimes depicted only as inscriptions. * '''{{ill|Thousand-armed Kannon|ja|千手観音|1=Thousand-armed Kannon|2=ja|3=千手観音|lt=Thousand-Armed Kannon|4=fr|5=Thousand-armed Kannon|6=ko|7=관세음보살|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Thousand-armed Kannon|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''' Characterized by 40 hands holding various implements and eyes in each palm to symbolize universal compassion. * '''[[:en:Cintāmaṇicakra|Nyoirin Kannon]]''' Typically depicted seated with a finger to the cheek in contemplation, holding a [[:en:Globus cruciger|Globus cruciger]] and a wheel of law. * '''[[:en:Dōsojin|Dōsojin]]''' Placed at village boundaries or bridges to ward off misfortune and promote blessings such as fertility, marital harmony, and safe travel. * '''{{ill|Kōshin-tō|ja|庚申塔|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōshin-tō|qq=}}''' Steles associated with [[:en:Kōshin|Kōshin faith]], often adorned with imagery of {{ill|Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko Ōkami|lt=Sarutahiko|ja|猿田彦神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarutahiko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to サルタヒコ}} or the [[:en:Three wise monkeys|three wise monkeys]].
== Notable Sekibutsu == * '''Fukushima Prefecture''' – [[:en:Daihisan Stone Buddhas|Daibisan no Sekibutsu]] * '''Nagano Prefecture''' – {{ill|Manji Stone Buddha|ja|万治の石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manji Stone Buddha|qq=}} * '''Kyoto Prefecture''' – {{ill|Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|ja|当尾磨崖仏文化財環境保全地区|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tono Magaibutsu Cultural Preservation Area|1=Tōno Stone Buddha Area|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11487822}} * '''Nara Prefecture''' – {{ill|Asuka Stone Carvings|ja|飛鳥の石造物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka Stone Carvings|qq=}}, {{ill|Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja|石位寺三尊石仏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekibutsu at Kōyadera|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} * '''Hyōgo Prefecture''' – {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|1=Rakan-ji Temple, Kasai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11608594}} * '''Ōita Prefecture''' – [[:en:Usuki Stone Buddhas|Usuki Stone Buddhas]], [[:en:Kumano Magaibutsu|Kumano Magaibutsu]]
== Gallery == <gallery> File:Angkor Thom (6648411831).jpg|[[:en:Angkor Thom|Angkor Thom]] in Cambodia File:Ajantha1.jpg|[[:en:Ajanta Caves|Ajanta Caves]] File:Saruishi 1940.jpg|{{ill|Saruishi|ja|猿石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saruishi|qq=}} (Asuka, Nara Prefecture) File:Onodera magaibutsu.jpg|Miroku Magaibutsu at [[:en:Ōno-ji|Ōnōdera]] File:Gohyakurakan Hyōgo-Kasai.jpg|Five Hundred Arhats at {{ill|Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|ja|羅漢寺 (加西市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rakanji Temple (Kasai)|qq=}} </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== See also == * [[:en:Ishibotoke Station|Ishibutsu Station]] * [[:en:Arhat|Arhat]] * {{ill|Kohouke Natural Park|ja|古法華自然公園|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kohouke Natural Park|1=Furubokke Nature Park|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11411414}} – Features numerous Sekibutsu, Magaibutsu, and Arhat carvings.
== External links == * [https://sekibutukyokai.jp/ Japanese Stone Buddha Association]
{{translated page|ja|石仏|version=96005561|comment=Imported full ja history}}

This article focuses on Japanese Sekibutsu.
[[File:Usuki Stone Buddhas.jpg|200px|thumb|Statue of Dainichi Nyorai ({{ill|Usuki Stone Buddhas|en|Usuki Stone Buddhas|WD=Q2921458|lt=Usuki Stone Buddhas}})]]
== Overview == From {{ill|Ancient history|en|Ancient history|WD=Q41493|lt=Ancient Japan}} to the {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|WD=Q12554|lt=Medieval Japan}}, numerous Sekibutsu were created, particularly in the {{ill|Kansai region|ja|関西地方|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kansai region|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 関西|1=Kansai|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1111292}}, commissioned by powerful patrons, {{ill|Shugendō|lt=Shugendō practitioners|ja|修験者|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shugendō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 山伏}}, and monks. By the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, stone Buddha carvings were increasingly made by common people as an expression of their faith, with diverse styles emerging in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kantō region|1=Kantō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11655841}}. Approximately 80% of extant Sekibutsu are believed to date from the Edo period. In modern times, Sekibutsu continue to appear in {{ill|Cemetery|en|Cemetery|WD=Q39614|lt=cemeteries}} or at sites of natural disasters, accidents, or murders as memorials, often in the form of {{ill|Kṣitigarbha|en|Kṣitigarbha|WD=Q604687|lt=Jizō Bosatsu}}.
